retrieved_docs.title: Bone Age -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Recently developed software computes bone age by GP and TW2 methods, which has demonstrated validity across different ethnicities.

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Age -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Visualization by ultrasound: Specialized ultrasound (US) device involving 2 transducers: one transmitted (ultrasonic waves of 750 kHz Frequency) and one receiver. Includes 11 cycles directed at the epiphysis of distal radius and ulna, and skeletal age is determined, based on demographics of subject and ultrasound results. Disadvantage: Still needs to be evaluated in multiethnic populations with a large sample size. [11] [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Conduction Evaluation -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple mechanisms are involved in bone conduction sound transmission, including the inertial force affecting cochlear fluids and middle ear ossicles, pressure changes in the ear canal, and pressure changes transmitted through a third window of the cochlea (which is a pathologic, abnormal structure). [2] Ultimately, air conduction and bone conduction cause a vibration of the cochlea's basilar membrane, a structure attached medially to the osseous spiral lamina, resulting in cochlear nerve stimulation. [3] Methods for testing bone conduction have existed since the 19th century. Early methods involved tuning forks and including the Weber and Rinne tests, which are still used today. [4] Modern bone conduction evaluation is frequently performed as a component of audiometry testing, especially when it is clinically useful to distinguish between sensorineural and conductive hearing loss. Bone conduction evaluation methods involve using specialized equipment, including an oscillator, to produce vibrations at predetermined frequencies and amplitudes. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Osseointegrated Bone-Conducting Hearing Protheses -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: There are two types of conventional hearing aids; air conduction hearing aids and bone conduction hearing aids. Air conductive hearing aids are a medical device that consists of a microphone, amplifier, and transducer that works as a loudspeaker in the external auditory canal. Bone conductive hearing aids consist and function similarly to air conductive hearing aids but differ in how the transducer function and where it is placed. In this type of hearing aids, the transducer work as bone oscillators that transmits vibration to the inner ear from the sound recollected by the external microphone. To effectively transduce the sound into a vibration, a medical device needs to be located near the temporal bone. The hearing aid is often placed near the temporal bone using headband or eyeglasses pieces. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Conduction Evaluation -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Bone conduction refers to the phenomenon in which vibrations are transmitted through the bones of the skull to the cochlea and the associated sensorineural structures, resulting in the perception of sound. Bone conduction is in contrast to the route of sound transmission known as air conduction, in which sound is transmitted in the air through the ear canal to the ossicles of the middle ear (malleus, incus, stapes) via the tympanic membrane, thus stimulating the sensorineural organs of the inner ear. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Conduction Evaluation -- Procedures -- Pure Tone Audiometry
retrieved_docs.content: Although audiometry is sometimes performed solely using earphones, which rely largely on AC, bone conduction can also be evaluated during audiometry and is an essential component of a true comprehensive audiogram. This is accomplished by positioning an oscillator on the patient’s mastoid process and measuring threshold values for the same frequencies used in air conduction testing. In this manner, bone conduction can be compared with air conduction graphically, illustrating what is referred to as an air-bone gap to document differences in air conduction vs bone conduction. Frequently, results from the right ear are plotted as an “O” for air conduction thresholds and an “<” for bone conduction thresholds, while results from the left ear are plotted as an “X” for air conduction thresholds and an “>” for bone conduction thresholds. [11] Often, the non-test ear is intentionally exposed to a masking frequency that is out of phase from the tone generated by the oscillator in the test ear, thus creating a noise-canceling effect. This masking technique is intended to prevent the oscillator's sound from being perceived by the non-test ear. This would skew test results, especially if the non-test ear has better acuity – and thus a lower threshold – at certain frequencies than the test ear. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Osseointegrated Bone-Conducting Hearing Protheses -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hearing loss is a common worldwide health concern that increases in both prevalence and severity with age. Individuals suffering from hearing loss often complain of having trouble communicating with poor speech recognition and localization of sounds at the work environment or social gatherings. Elderly patients with hearing loss are at higher risk for social isolation, lack of independence, and early dementia.  Treatment for hearing loss often involved sound amplification medical devices called conventional hearing aids. Conventional hearing aids are commonly used as the main auditory rehabilitation tool because they are non-invasive and less expensive treatments available. The medical device consists of a microphone, amplifier, and loudspeaker. Its main function is to raise the volume or magnify the sound in our environment. While its positive impact on patients' quality of life, including communication, social, emotional, and cognitive function, has been proven, most patients are non-compliant because of the non-ideal features. These features include insufficient amplification, acoustic feedback, lack of directionality, occlusion effects. Conventional hearing aids have been forced to evolve since they were first developed to adjust to patient aesthetic and functional demands. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Conduction Evaluation -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Factors that may interfere with the successful and accurate evaluation of bone conduction are numerous and complex. Some evidence suggests that manual (also known as traditional) PTA methods) air-bone gaps are more susceptible to tester bias effects than those obtained via automated PTA methods. This evidence is based on large datasets showing lower air-bone gap variability in manual versus automated PTA. It is hypothesized to be associated with prior knowledge of hearing loss characteristics. [38] Other studies suggest that manual and automated PTA methods produce sufficiently similar results, indicating that either method is acceptable, provided their calculated threshold differences fall within approximately 5 dB of each other. [39] [40] It has been hypothesized that frequencies above 4,000 Hz are prone to error from false air-bone gaps during PTA. [41] Occlusion of the ear canal during bone conduction evaluation does not appear to have a significant corrective effect on these false air-bone gaps. [42] However, several studies have suggested that adjusting the reference equivalent threshold force level, a value used in determining bone conduction thresholds, by approximately 14 dB proves to be effective at correcting for false air-bone gaps at these frequencies. [43]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bone Markings -- Structure and Function -- Common Bone Markings
retrieved_docs.content: Meatus: A tube-like channel that extends within the bone, which may provide passage and protection to nerves and vessels. The external and internal acoustic meatus accommodate sound transmission (see Image . External Ear, Horizontal Section).

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Age -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Bone age assessment can be of utmost importance in various complicated situations involving medical, surgical, forensic, and legal issues. Apart from the ability to assess the chronological age of a person, the evaluation of growth remaining in a person can be extremely beneficial to a clinician in different circumstances, as previously discussed. Although the role of hand x-ray in skeletal maturity estimation is well-known, evaluation of multiple other radiographs can be more beneficial in certain circumstances. The evidence on this subject is still not extensive, and future research can pave the way for a better understanding of each of these skeletal assessment modalities. Future research can also help us with developing newer modalities, that do not involving significant radiation exposure for regular assessment of bone age in young children and adolescents. Also, more automated software to assess bone age can revolutionize this field substantially.

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Conduction Evaluation -- Procedures -- Auditory Brainstem Response
retrieved_docs.content: As an objective testing method, ABR testing has the advantage of being useful in evaluating hearing in infants, young children, and other patients with limited ability to communicate. As with PTA, ABR testing is usually performed with earphones, thus involving air conduction through transforming mechanical vibration in the ear canal into bioelectrical signals via cochlear hair cells. However, because air conduction and bone conduction result in stimulation of the cochlear nerve (cranial nerve VIII), bone conduction can also be evaluated in the stimulation of ABR. Evaluating bone conduction in this manner is accomplished by using an oscillator in contact with the mastoid process or other bony structures of the skull. [17] In comparing PTA with ABR testing in the context of bone conduction stimulation, a difference in threshold level of 18 to 28 dB (decibels) can be expected in individuals with normal hearing, with ABR registering at a higher threshold than PTA. [18] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Age -- Introduction -- Techniques Used to Determine Bone Age
retrieved_docs.content: Visualization by hand and wrist plain film radiographs: Standard posteroanterior (PA) view of the hand and wrist; Effective radiation received during each exposure is between 0.0001-0.1 mSV. [1] Typically, left-hand radiographs are utilized (as most individuals are right dominant and the left hand has less chance of being deformed)

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Age -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Tanner Whitehouse Method: Based on the level of maturity for 20 selected regions of interest (ROI) in specific bones of hand and wrist in each age population. The development level for each ROI is categorized into specific stages, and given a numerical score for each individual bone. A total maturity score is calculated by summing up all these scores, which then correlates with sex-specific bone age. Advantage: More accurate and more reproducible. Disadvantage: Comparatively more complex and more time-consuming. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Age -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Visualization of iliac bone: By pelvic radiographs: Risser sign: Based on the ossification of iliac crest apophysis. However, not a reliable or commonly used technique. [21] [22] Non-ionizing Imaging: Ultrasonography as a technique to assess bone age using iliac crest apophysis ossification has also been considered. However, this technique still requires validation.

retrieved_docs.content: effects as specific conditions associated with the concept of “كسر,” situates them within a broader category of unspecified injury sequelae, and further delineates limb‑specific fracture sequelae, thereby providing a structured framework for understanding and managing the long‑term consequences of various bone injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 362
retrieved_docs.content: Other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities are associated with bone resorption, indicating that these conditions often involve increased breakdown of bone tissue, which contributes to skeletal weakening and structural abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 203
retrieved_docs.content: of closed fractures and various fixation techniques is tightly connected to osteoporosis‑related bone fragility. This integrated framework supports comprehensive patient assessment, informed surgical planning, and coordinated management of fractures and bone‑density disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1907
retrieved_docs.content: الالْتِهاب (inflammation) is associated with other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities, indicating that inflammatory processes can contribute to the development or worsening of skeletal disorders and structural abnormalities of the musculoskeletal system. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as addressing underlying inflammation may help prevent or mitigate bone pathology and deformities, thereby improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1899
retrieved_docs.content: MISCELLANEOUS DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC PROCEDURES is a broad classification that encompasses a variety of diagnostic and therapeutic interventions, and within this category the specific procedure صورة نخاعية للمنطقة الشوكية is listed as a subordinate (child) procedure, indicating that spinal‑region imaging is considered one of the miscellaneous diagnostic and therapeutic techniques.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 230
retrieved_docs.content: hip biomechanics. Additionally, “Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction” is identified as a versatile internal fixation technique applicable to fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur, the radius and ulna, and other lower‑extremity sites, underscoring its role across multiple skeletal regions. Collectively, these entities illustrate an integrated framework for fracture fixation, deformity correction, growth modulation, prosthetic revision, and preventive care in thigh and leg surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 861
retrieved_docs.content: and are interwoven with radiology services, biomedical imaging research, cross‑sectional anatomical study, and computer‑assisted surgical technologies. This interconnected framework supports comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and precision‑guided therapeutic interventions in modern medical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 189
retrieved_docs.content: relationships form a structured network in which fractures—whether of multiple bones, the spine, trunk, limbs, femoral neck, or skull vault—are intricately linked to specific late‑effect conditions, anatomical locations, treatment morphologies, broader injury classifications, and associated behavioral factors, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding fracture sequelae and related injuries in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2107
retrieved_docs.content: Skull and face fractures, described in Arabic as كسر في قبو القحف, refer to injuries involving breaks in the cranial vault and the facial bones, encompassing a range of traumatic damage to the head and facial structures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1918
retrieved_docs.content: The radius is identified as an allowed qualifier in several specialized domains, indicating that it can be the focus of detailed study or intervention in each area. In ultrastructural research, the radius is an allowed qualifier, meaning that subcellular imaging and analysis are applicable to this bone. Anatomical and histological investigations also list the radius as an allowed qualifier, reflecting its significance in the study of bone structure and tissue composition. Cytological assessment includes the radius as an allowed qualifier, allowing cellular‑level examination of bone cells. In the context of transplantation, the radius is an allowed qualifier, signifying that it may be considered for grafting or transplant procedures. Clinically, the radius is the direct_procedure_site_of treatment for fractures or dislocations of the radius and ulna, highlighting its central role in orthopedic care and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 641
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium supplements (مكملات الكالسيوم) are intended for therapeutic use, meaning they are administered to treat or prevent medical conditions related to calcium deficiency or bone health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 983
retrieved_docs.content: The condition referred to as “اعتلال مرتكز العظم” functions as a parent category that includes spondylosis, intervertebral disc disorders, and other back problems. These specific spinal and disc pathologies are therefore understood as manifestations of the broader bone‑centered disease entity, allowing clinicians to classify and address them within a unified diagnostic and therapeutic framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 462
retrieved_docs.content: Other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities are closely linked to inflammation (الالْتِهاب) and to bone resorption (resorpce kosti). These associations indicate that inflammatory processes and the loss of bone tissue are central mechanisms underlying a range of skeletal disorders and deformities. Recognizing the connection between inflammation and bone resorption can guide diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at preserving bone integrity and preventing further musculoskeletal complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2023
retrieved_docs.content: Various late effects of bone injuries are directly linked to the underlying fracture. The “Late effect of fracture of multiple bones,” the “Late gevolgen van fractuur van schedel en aangezichtsbeenderen” (late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones), the Arabic phrases “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر الأطراف السفلية” (late effects of fracture of the lower limbs), “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر الأطراف العلوية” (late effects of fracture of the upper limbs), and “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر عنق فخذ” (late effects of fracture of the femoral neck) are all associated with “كسر,” the Arabic term for fracture. This relationship underscores that regardless of the anatomical region—whether multiple bones, the skull and facial skeleton, the upper or lower extremities, or the femoral neck—post‑traumatic sequelae are fundamentally connected to the original fracture event, highlighting the importance of monitoring and managing these late complications in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 877
retrieved_docs.content: The late effect of fracture of multiple bones, the late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones, and the late effects of fracture of the femoral neck are all described as being associated with a fracture (كسر). In addition, the open reduction of an open shoulder dislocation with fracture of the greater tuberosity is identified as a direct morphological manifestation of a fracture (كسر). Together, these statements highlight that both delayed sequelae across various skeletal regions and specific surgical interventions are directly linked to the underlying presence of a fracture, emphasizing the need for comprehensive assessment and management of fracture‑related pathology in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1174
retrieved_docs.content: shared therapeutic goals for musculoskeletal and connective‑tissue conditions. This integrated framework supports comprehensive patient care by aligning specific modalities with appropriate clinical indications.
